provenance.source_corpus: Soil carbon may be maintained under grazing in a St Lawrence Estuary tidal marsh

source_spans.text: Grazing reduces plant litter and increases solar exposure, soil temperature (at this latitude, soil remained frozen until April) and evapotranspiration, thus raising soil salinity and nitrogen demand, the latter a driver of root production.
The grazed marsh had higher soil carbon density and belowground production
The grazed marsh had higher soil carbon density and belowground production
The grazed marsh had higher soil carbon density and belowground production, yet lower aboveground biomass.
Grazing reduces plant litter
Grazing reduces plant litter and increases solar exposure, soil temperature (at this latitude, soil remained frozen until April) and evapotranspiration, thus raising soil salinity and nitrogen demand
Grazing reduces plant litter and increases solar exposure, soil temperature (at this latitude, soil remained frozen until April) and evapotranspiration, thus raising soil salinity and nitrogen demand
The average depth to water table in each month was lower (F 1,4 = 15.5, p = 0.017; Fig. 2) in the grazed than the ungrazed area.
Our results suggest that, in cold climate marshes, controlled grazing may increase the organic matter content of the soil

Our results suggest that, in cold climate marshes, controlled grazing may increase the organic matter content of the soil, and may thus enhance the vertical accretion required for marsh sustainability
Grazing reduces plant litter and increases solar exposure, soil temperature (at this latitude, soil remained frozen until April) and evapotranspiration, thus raising soil salinity and nitrogen demand
